output:
# PIVOT over TRUSTLOCK: Per-Instance Posterior Interpolation Against the Trust-Allocation Lock of In-Context Classification

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Large language models are pre-trained either as masked language models (MLM) that
predict randomly masked tokens (BERT [1], RoBERTa [2], BART [3]) or as
auto-regressive/causal language models (CLM) that predict a token from its
predecessors (GPT-2 [4], GPT-3 [5], LLaMA [6], Mistral [7]). Scaled to billions of
parameters and instruction-tuned [15], they adapt to unseen tasks from a few
in-prompt examples — in-context learning (ICL) [5]. For the predictive task of text
classification, each class is specified by a VERBALISER (a set of label words), and
generated text is mapped to a class through the posterior the frozen decoder places
over those verbaliser tokens [9].

This deploys a single frozen-parameter readout: one verbaliser posterior (softmax
over fixed label-word tokens) judged at one fixed decision rule, with no parameter
updates [5,9]. As the source intro observes, ICL is conceptually a non-parametric,
k-NN-like predictor whose decision depends on the LOCAL TOPOLOGY of the few-shot
examples — but with a frozen LLM doing the mixing. Crucially, that neighbourhood is
never MATERIALISED as a separate object: it stays latent inside the single
parametric posterior. (This ICL-as-kNN / local-regression view is an established
reading, supported by the explicit-kNN line [10,14], not a single canonical source.)

Five literatures each solve a PART of the problem this opens, but none composes the
whole:
- **Demonstration selection/retrieval.** KATE [11] and EPR [12] retrieve nearer
  neighbours into the prompt, but the readout remains a single parametric posterior;
  the neighbourhood never becomes an explicit competing posterior.
- **Explicit non-parametric posteriors.** kNN-LM [10], kNN-Prompting [14], and
  adaptive kNN-LM variants [16,17] build an EXPLICIT non-parametric posterior and
  interpolate it with the model — but they mix with a GLOBAL weight λ or a TRAINED
  gate, at the token-LM layer, with no per-instance disagreement gate and no exact
  decision-for-decision collapse. This is the strongest near-miss.
- **ICL calibration.** Contextual Calibration [13], PMI/noisy-channel prompting [8],
  and label-bias corrections [18,19] globally re-scale the SAME single posterior; no
  second non-parametric posterior, no per-instance trust.
- **Verbaliser/prompt-based classification.** PET [9] defines classes by label words
  but fine-tunes and uses a global verbaliser; no frozen readout arbitration over
  local topology.
- **Per-instance gating / reliability signals.** Mixture-of-experts gating and OOD
  reliability scores (e.g. Mahalanobis [20]) exist generically, but were never wired
  to a frozen-LLM verbaliser readout with a disagreement gate and an exact-collapse
  default.

The precise opening: the implicit kNN of ICL is never made EXPLICIT, never
PER-INSTANCE-MIXED with the parametric posterior, and never EXACTLY-COLLAPSING back
to the deployed decision. That triad is the gap this work names and fills.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

**The gap — TRUSTLOCK (Trust-allocation Lock).** The deployed ICL readout is a single
verbaliser posterior judged at one fixed rule. It implicitly performs kNN over the
few-shot examples, but COMMITS to one global, instance-independent allocation of
trust between the parametric prior and the local neighbourhood; it cannot express,
per instance, how much to trust local topology versus the parametric posterior. The
harm is SILENT under pooled accuracy because it concentrates exactly where the
parametric posterior and the local kNN topology DISAGREE — boundary / low-purity
neighbourhoods, sparse / low-density regions, minority verbaliser classes, and
demonstration-order/selection-sensitive queries — a measure-small, near-zero-mean
slice that cancels in the pool average. It VANISHES when the two posteriors agree
everywhere (high purity, dense neighbourhoods, no minority gap): there any trust
weight yields the same decision and the lock costs nothing.

**The method — PIVOT (Posterior Interpolation Via Observable Topology).** With the
LLM FROZEN, PIVOT forms two posteriors over the C classes: (a) the parametric
verbaliser posterior `p_LLM(c|x)` from the decoder; (b) an EXPLICIT non-parametric
kNN posterior `p_kNN(c|x)` computed by leave-one-out (LOO) over the SAME few-shot
support set in the frozen LLM's own representation space,
`p_kNN(c|x) ∝ Σ_{i∈N_k(x)} w_i·1[y_i=c]`, with `w_i = softmax(−d_i/h)`. It combines
them purely at the SCORE/DECISION layer by a per-instance log-linear interpolation
`f(c|x) = log p_LLM(c|x) + a(x)·log p_kNN(c|x)`, `ŷ = argmax_c f(c|x)`. The trust
weight `a(x) ∈ [0,1]` is produced by a monotone GATE on observable local-topology
signals: neighbourhood label PURITY, neighbour DISTANCE/DENSITY, and — the new axis —
parametric-vs-neighbour DISAGREEMENT (cross-entropy between `p_LLM` and the
neighbour-label distribution). The gate raises `a(x)` only in unreliable-parametric /
high-disagreement neighbourhoods and clamps it toward 0 elsewhere.

**Exact-collapse guarantee.** At the gate's identity default `a(x) ≡ 0`,
`f(c|x) = log p_LLM(c|x)` for every class and query, so PIVOT reproduces the deployed
ICL verbaliser-posterior decision DECISION-FOR-DECISION. The collapse error
`E_collapse = max over the eval batch of |f(c|x)|_{a=0} − log p_LLM(c|x)|` is 0 by
construction; every build asserts `E_collapse ≤ 1e-6`. Scope: the identity holds to
FLOATING-POINT tolerance (float32/16) and is verified in SHADOW MODE against an
independently recomputed vanilla-ICL path. It is a do-no-harm safety floor — turning
PIVOT on can only ADD corrections in disagreement regions, guaranteeing zero
pooled-accuracy regression — not a claim of analytic exactness beyond float tolerance.

**Novelty wedge (C1–C5).** PIVOT is the conjunction C1∧C2∧C3∧C4∧C5: **C1** frozen LLM,
pure score layer (no fine-tuning); **C2** an EXPLICIT LOO kNN class posterior in the
LLM's own representation space; **C3** a PER-INSTANCE interpolation weight (not a
global λ); **C4** a gate on observable local topology INCLUDING parametric-vs-neighbour
DISAGREEMENT, plus purity and distance/density; **C5** provable exact collapse to
vanilla ICL at `a≡0`. No prior family meets all five — the explicit-kNN line owns C1–C2
but misses the per-instance disagreement gate and the decision-for-decision identity.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?** — Can replacing vanilla verbaliser-based
in-context learning's single, instance-independent reliance on the frozen LLM's
parametric posterior with PIVOT — a frozen-LLM, score-layer interpolation between that
verbaliser posterior and an explicit leave-one-out kNN class posterior built over the
same few-shot support set in the LLM's representation space, mixed by a per-instance
weight gated by observable local-topology signals (neighbourhood label purity,
distance/density, and parametric-vs-neighbour disagreement) — improve text-
classification accuracy in neighbourhoods where the parametric posterior and the local
topology disagree while provably collapsing decision-for-decision to vanilla ICL at the
gate's identity default with E_collapse <= 1e-6?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

*P1 — Deployment reality.* ICL is the dominant few-shot deployment mode for frozen
LLMs. PIVOT acts at the score layer using the same support and the same
representations, so it ships against closed/API models where retraining approaches —
PET fine-tuning [9] or a learned EPR retriever [12] — cannot be applied at all. It is
zero-retraining, model-agnostic, and drop-in.

*P2 — Silent tail harm.* A single global trust allocation errs precisely where
`p_LLM` and the local kNN topology disagree: boundary/low-purity, sparse/low-density,
and minority verbaliser-class regions. Because that residual is near-zero-mean, it
CANCELS in pooled accuracy and is invisible to the standard yardstick. PIVOT's
explicit `p_kNN`-vs-`p_LLM` cross-entropy NAMES the harmed slice, pushing evaluation
toward disagreement-stratified reporting that can actually see the loss.

*P3 — Order/selection robustness.* Demonstration order and selection sensitivity is a
well-documented ICL pathology. PIVOT adds an explicit LOO neighbourhood posterior so
the gate can lean on local topology exactly when the parametric readout is unstable;
this is complementary to, not competitive with, retrieval methods like KATE/EPR
[11,12].

*P4 — Minority/fairness.* Minority verbaliser classes are under-served by the
parametric prior. The purity/density gate makes minority-region behaviour an
auditable function of observable topology rather than an opaque artefact of the
prompt, and stratified minority macro-F1 exposes whether they are rescued.

*P5 — Safety / do-no-harm.* The exact collapse (`a≡0 ⇒ f = log p_LLM`, decision-for-
decision, `E_collapse ≤ 1e-6`, float/shadow scope) makes the worst case provably "no
change." This converts an accuracy-versus-risk trade-off into a bounded-downside
shadow rollout — a guarantee no global-mixing or global-calibration baseline offers.

*P6 — Interpretable trust.* The per-instance weight `a(x)` together with the measured
disagreement is itself a triage/abstention signal: high disagreement combined with low
purity is a principled cue to route an instance to human review.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

*O1 — The binding obstacle (representational, not tuning).* PIVOT must construct THREE
coupled objects — the kNN posterior `p_kNN`, the disagreement signal, and the gate
`a(x)` — from the SAME tiny few-shot support, with no extra labels, no training, and
without ever degrading the deployed decision. The gate is self-referential: its inputs
are high-variance statistics of a k-point sample, and at 1–8 examples per class the
sampling noise is on the order of the disagreement it must detect. Naive fixes
provably fail. A global λ is the wrong functional form — it cannot be simultaneously
correct in high-purity-dense regions and boundary-sparse regions. A free per-bin fit
is unidentifiable at small k: bin assignment is itself noisy, overfits the support,
and inverts on test. Using the support twice leaks — a self-neighbour at distance 0
fakes perfect purity and agreement. The escape is forced: LOO estimation of `p_kNN`;
a gate calibrated by LOO out-of-sample agreement; a monotone, clamped gate with a hard
identity default `a≡0` so corrections only ADD where reliability is strong; and
reliability-threshold shrinkage.

*O2 — Representation geometry.* Transformer hidden states are anisotropic; layer and
pooling choices change the topology and therefore the gate inputs. This forces
mean-pooling plus whitening/Mahalanobis distance and a layer sweep.

*O3 — Posterior commensurability.* The verbaliser log-prob scales are not directly
commensurable with kNN evidence, and majority-token bias distorts them. This forces
length normalisation and comparison against already-calibrated baselines so that any
lift is not merely a re-derivation of contextual calibration.

*O4 — Metric blindness.* Pooled accuracy structurally cannot see either the harm or
the fix. This forces stratified metrics (purity/density/disagreement), minority
macro-F1, order-sensitivity variance, and a do-no-harm equivalence test.

*O5 — Monotonicity/identity vs expressiveness.* The deliberately less-expressive,
monotone gate family is what buys exact collapse and identifiability — so the
ablations must demonstrate that a per-instance gate STILL beats a global λ within that
constrained family.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?** — Each adjacent literature owns
only a fragment of the conjunction C1∧C2∧C3∧C4∧C5, and the pieces were never composed
with a disagreement gate and an exact-collapse default. KATE/EPR [11,12] change the
inputs but keep a single parametric readout, missing C2–C5. ICL calibration
[13,8,19,18] applies a global affine/prior correction to one posterior, missing
C2,C3,C4,C5. PET [9] fine-tunes with a global verbaliser, missing C1–C5. Generic
MoE/gating with reliability scores [20] was never wired to a frozen-LLM verbaliser
readout in this specific form. The STRONGEST near-miss — and the honest collapse-
adjacent baseline — is the explicit-kNN line: kNN-LM [10], kNN-Prompting [14], and
adaptive kNN-LM [16,17] genuinely own C1 and C2 (a frozen model interpolated with an
explicit non-parametric posterior), but they mix with a GLOBAL λ or a TRAINED gate at
the token-LM layer, so they miss C3 (per-instance weight) and C4 (a disagreement
gate), and they provide no C5 decision-for-decision identity default. We do NOT claim
explicit posteriors, neighbour selection, calibration, the verbaliser readout, or
per-instance gating in general; we claim only their CONJUNCTION with a
parametric-vs-neighbour disagreement gate and a provable exact collapse — which no
existing line provides.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

*Method.* `p_LLM` comes from verbaliser-token log-probs (single-token: log-softmax
over the verbaliser set; multi-token: length-normalised, then renormalised over
classes), byte-identical to vanilla ICL's decision input. Representations are hidden
states at layer ℓ (~⅔ depth by default, swept), mean-pooled over the span to `z`, with
optional whitening `z̃ = Σ^(−1/2)(z − μ)` from the support pool so Euclidean distance
equals Mahalanobis distance (fixing anisotropy, O2). The LOO kNN uses `N_k(x)` by
whitened distance, `w_i = softmax(−d_i/h)`, `p_kNN(c|x) ∝ Σ_{i∈N_k} w_i·1[y_i=c]`,
with `h` set from the neighbour-distance scale (median) or LOO-validated; LOO is
mandatory for support points (no self-neighbour). Gate signals from `N_k`: purity
`P = max_c Σ w_i·1[y_i=c]`; density `D` (monotone-decreasing in neighbour distance);
disagreement `G = H(p_neigh, p_LLM)`. The gate is
`a(x) = a_max·σ(β_P·s(P) + β_D·s(D) + β_G·s(G) − τ)` with all `β ≥ 0` (monotone),
clamped to `[0, a_max]`, and shrunk to 0 if `P < P_min` or `D < D_min`; identity
default `a ≡ 0`. The parameters `{β, τ, a_max}` are fit WITHOUT extra labels via LOO
reliability on the support (label = did adding kNN trust flip `x_i` toward its true
`y_i`), using constrained logistic/isotonic regression with a do-no-harm penalty;
fallbacks are a tiny held-out support split or unsupervised purity/density-quantile
calibration. Combine: `f(c|x) = log p_LLM(c|x) + a(x)·log p_kNN(c|x)`, `ŷ = argmax_c f`.

*Collapse harness.* At `a ≡ 0`, assert `max|f − log p_LLM| ≤ 1e-6` and decision-for-
decision identity against an INDEPENDENTLY recomputed vanilla-ICL path, in shadow
mode, scoped to float32/16 tolerance.

*Datasets.* SST-2, SST-5, AG News, TREC (6-way), DBpedia-14, Yahoo! Answers (10-way),
RTE, CB, plus an induced class-imbalanced variant; shots 1/2/4/8 per class (support
sizes reported).

*Models (frozen).* GPT-2-XL 1.5B [4], Llama-2-7B and Llama-2-13B [6], Mistral-7B [7];
model-agnostic, replicated across ≥3/4.

*Baselines (shot/compute-matched).* Vanilla ICL (the collapse target), Contextual
Calibration [13], domain/PMI calibration [8,19], kNN-Prompting [14], a kNN-LM
global-λ variant = PIVOT with constant `a` (the key contrast for C3), KATE [11], and a
majority-class floor.

*Metrics.* Pooled accuracy and macro-F1; STRATIFIED accuracy/F1 by purity decile,
density bin, and disagreement bin; minority macro-F1; ECE [21]; order-sensitivity
variance (accuracy std over ~10 permutations); and a do-no-harm TOST equivalence test
at the identity default.

*Ablations / falsifier ladder.* (1) gate-off collapse must equal vanilla; (2) global-λ
vs per-instance gate (isolates C3); (3) each gate signal alone vs combined (isolates
C4, with disagreement expected to be the strongest single driver); (4) PLACEBO with
shuffled neighbour labels — the lift must vanish (primary falsifier); (5) layer/whitening
sweep; (6) k,h sensitivity; (7) dose-response — lift should grow with disagreement-bin
severity; (8) LOO vs train-pool retrieval should agree, while no-LOO inflates (leakage
proof). DECISIVE FALSIFIER: if the lift does not concentrate in high-disagreement /
low-purity / low-density / minority bins, or does not survive the placebo, or is matched
by global-λ, the mechanism is refuted and any gain is reattributed to generic
calibration.

*Feasibility.* All frozen inference, zero training: one forward pass plus a cheap kNN
over a tiny support set plus a closed-form gate, on 1–2 GPUs. Key risks (representation
quality, tiny-support variance, scale mismatch, metric invisibility) are each mitigated
(whitening/layer sweep; LOO + shrinkage; length-norm + calibrated baselines; stratified
metrics).

*Expected outcomes (direction only, no fabricated magnitudes).* Near-zero pooled
regression (from collapse plus clamping) with the TOST equivalence passing; gains
CONCENTRATED in high-disagreement, low-purity, and minority bins together with reduced
order-sensitivity, exhibiting a dose-response signature; the per-instance gate beating
global-λ in the tail, disagreement the strongest single driver, and the placebo killing
the lift.

## References and Evidence

[1] Devlin et al. 2019. BERT. NAACL-HLT.
[2] Liu et al. 2019. RoBERTa. arXiv.
[3] Lewis et al. 2020. BART. ACL.
[4] Radford et al. 2019. GPT-2. OpenAI technical report.
[5] Brown et al. 2020. GPT-3 / Language Models are Few-Shot Learners. NeurIPS.
[6] Touvron et al. 2023. LLaMA. arXiv.
[7] Jiang et al. 2023. Mistral 7B. arXiv.
[8] Min et al. 2022. Noisy Channel Language Model Prompting. ACL (and Min et al. 2022,
    Rethinking the Role of Demonstrations, EMNLP).
[9] Schick & Schütze 2021. PET / Exploiting Cloze Questions. EACL.
[10] Khandelwal et al. 2020. kNN-LM / Generalization through Memorization. ICLR.
[11] Liu et al. 2022. KATE / What Makes Good In-Context Examples for GPT-3? DeeLIO@ACL.
[12] Rubin et al. 2022. EPR / Learning to Retrieve Prompts for In-Context Learning.
     NAACL-HLT.
[13] Zhao et al. 2021. Contextual Calibration / Calibrate Before Use. ICML.
[14] Xu et al. 2023. kNN-Prompting. ICLR.
[15] Ouyang et al. 2022. InstructGPT / Training LMs to Follow Instructions. NeurIPS.
[16] He et al. 2021. Efficient Nearest Neighbor Language Models. EMNLP.
[17] Drozdov et al. 2022. When and How to Rely on Retrieval in the kNN-LM. Findings of
     EMNLP.
[18] Han et al. 2023. Prototypical Calibration for Few-Shot Learning of LMs. ICLR.
[19] Fei et al. 2023. Mitigating Label Biases for In-Context Learning. ACL.
[20] Lee et al. 2018. A Simple Unified Framework for Detecting OOD Samples (Mahalanobis).
     NeurIPS.
[21] Guo et al. 2017. On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks (ECE). ICML.

*Citation hygiene note:* the "ICL ≈ kNN / local regression over demonstrations" framing
is a known, established view supported by the explicit-kNN line [10,14]; it is not
attributed to any single fabricated source. All bracketed citations in the prose above
map to this list.
